MARKET.head: Collectible tequila market surges on auction record and investment interest
MARKET.summ: With tequila hitting a new world auction record in recent weeks, and WineFi offering irs first premium tequila investment barrels, is the agave spirit set for a new boom? Arabella Mileham reports
takeaways: Clase Azul Dia de Muertos bottle sold for US$35,000 at Sotheby's, becoming most expensive tequila ever auctioned.
Global tequila market valued at US$12.6bn, projected to reach US$21.6bn by 2033 with 9% growth.
WineFi launched premium tequila investment fund of 50 barrels, citing historic returns of 15–35% for comparable aged assets.
Younger consumers driving demand for premium and super-premium tequila, with whisky drinkers increasingly switching to agave spirits.
Extra Añejo tequila requires minimum three years maturation; supply remains constrained by regulation and agricultural timelines.

MARKET.head: Breaky Bottom, England's pioneering vineyard, sells for first time in 52 years
MARKET.summ: Breaky Bottom, one of the founding estates of the modern English wine movement, has been put up for sale for the first time since its establishment in 1974. The Sussex vineyard, created by late English wine pioneer Peter Hall, carries a guide price of £4 million
takeaways: Breaky Bottom, founded in 1974 by Peter Hall, is for sale at £4 million following Hall's death in October 2025.
The 15-acre Sussex estate produces around 10,000 bottles annually of sparkling wines from Seyval Blanc and Chardonnay-Pinot blends.
Hall's wines were served at King Charles III's coronation, Queen Elizabeth II's Platinum Jubilee, and the 2012 London Olympics.
The sale includes vineyard land, a four-bedroom farmhouse, traditional barn winery, and production facilities.
Knight Frank expects international buyer interest, citing resilient demand for small-scale, terroir-driven producers despite broader market challenges.

MARKET.head: Distiller Offers Solution for Excess Wine Inventory
MARKET.summ: Premium distiller has good news for excess inventory crowding cellar space and compromising profitability
takeaways: A premium distiller is addressing winemakers' cellar space and profitability challenges from surplus stock.
The distiller converts excess wine inventory into spirits, freeing up storage capacity.
The service targets producers dealing with inventory buildup that strains physical and financial resources.

MARKET.head: Japanese winery awards boost awareness but not always sales for small producers
MARKET.summ: Japanese wines have long been recognised by the likes of DWWA and IWSC, but for a country whose majority of wineries are mom-and-pop shops, do these accolades actually matter to producers looking to grow exports? Joyce Yip finds out
takeaways: Large Japanese producers like Château Mercian and Katsunuma Jyozo dominate international awards; small family wineries rarely enter competitions.
About 70 percent of Japan's 400-plus wineries are family-operated with limited production, prioritizing domestic sales over costly exports.
Awards provide commercial credibility: Katsunuma Jyozo's medals at Vinalies Internationales led to European distribution through Bernard Magrez Group.
Restaurant placement and high-profile exposure matter more than awards for driving sales; Château Mercian's 2016 G7 Summit service boosted credibility.
Award impact has diminished as wine critics and competitions proliferate globally; importers now rely on tastings and brand storytelling instead.

MARKET.head: Kosmic Kitty Wines expands distribution across major retail chains
MARKET.summ: July 10, 2026 (Rolling Hills, CA) , Kosmic Kitty Wines®, produced by South Bay Wine Group LLC, continues its rapid retail expansion with growing distribution across major supermarket chains and state-controlled
takeaways: South Bay Wine Group LLC produces Kosmic Kitty Wines brand.
The wine is expanding distribution into major supermarket chains.
Distribution also includes state-controlled alcohol markets.

CULTURE.head: London masterclass explores diverse rosé styles and producers
CULTURE.summ: Think you might be getting stuck in a rosé rut? Now you can find a pink wine that fits your present mood or preferred food at an evening masterclass in London on 10 September
takeaways: Tasting event on 10 September at The Chelsea Townhouse in London showcases varied rosé styles and brands.
Master of Wine Patrick Schmitt hosts the masterclass, limited to 28 guests, for all experience levels.
Event features both niche and mainstream rosé offerings to broaden drinkers' palate preferences.
Rosé producers gain direct access to consumers and promotional support through the drinks business and venue.

CULTURE.head: Pol Roger releases 2019 vintage amid challenging growing conditions
CULTURE.summ: Champagne Pol Roger has unveiled its latest vintage expression from the exceptional 2019 harvest, and Patrick Schmitt MW gives his verdict
takeaways: The 2019 harvest faced April frost, mildew, and August hailstorms, reducing regional yields by nearly 20%.
Optimal harvesting conditions produced wines with rare harmony, freshness, and exceptional fruit health.
The 2019 vintage marks cellar master Damien Cambres' first solo release, featuring 63.5% Pinot Noir and 36.5% Chardonnay.
Average alcohol potential reached 10.7%, exceeding the 2018 vintage's 10.2%, reflecting the vintage's power.
Aged six years in underground cellars, the wine displays citrus and stone fruit notes with aging potential of 3-5 years.

CULTURE.head: Sam Neill, Two Paddocks founder, dies aged 78
CULTURE.summ: Sam Neill, the actor behind Jurassic Park's Dr Alan Grant and founder of Central Otago winery Two Paddocks, has died aged 78. While celebrated across the world for his film career, he spent more than three decades quietly building one of New Zealand's most respected Pinot Noir estates
takeaways: Neill planted Two Paddocks' first vineyard in Central Otago in 1993 and spent over 30 years developing it into a benchmark Pinot Noir producer.
The winery expanded across four sites in Gibbston, Alexandra and Bannockburn, becoming the only Central Otago producer with vineyards in all three principal valleys.
Two Paddocks became certified organic from the 2017 vintage and earned international acclaim for restrained, ageworthy estate-grown and single vineyard bottlings.
Neill rejected the 'celebrity winemaker' label, arguing it diminished the wines, and deliberately kept production modest while championing Burgundian principles and screwcaps.
He died in Sydney aged 78 following a sudden illness, having previously announced he was cancer-free earlier this year after a 2023 diagnosis.

CULTURE.head: Nyetimber holds top spot as English wine field broadens dramatically
CULTURE.summ: Last year Nyetimber topped the charts in this comprehensive blind tasting of English wines carried out by Australian publication The Real Review. But has the Sussex producer managed to hold onto its crown in 2026, or has it been eclipsed by another contender? Anthony Rose reports
takeaways: Nyetimber retained its crown as top British winery for 2026, winning the title three times and in two consecutive years.
Coates & Seely surged from 12th to 3rd place, marking an almost unprecedented single-year jump in the rankings.
Hampshire emerged as the most dynamic county, with multiple producers improving standings including Raimes English Sparkling leaping 17 places to 14th.
Sussex, Kent, and Dorset producers dominate top positions, though geographic spread of quality wine production continues to expand.
The ranked list grew to 39 producers total, with ten new wineries entering, reflecting deepening talent depth across English sparkling wine.

CULTURE.head: Actor Sam Neill, New Zealand Winemaker, Dies at 78
CULTURE.summ: The beloved 'Jurassic Park' actor and New Zealand wine titan has died at 78. We’re sharing our conversation with the winemaker back in 2021
takeaways: Sam Neill, known for Jurassic Park, was also a significant figure in New Zealand wine production.
Neill died at age 78.
Winefeed previously interviewed Neill about his winemaking work in 2021.

SCIENCE.head: Pinord's Biodynamic Chardonnay Scores Highly in Wine Enthusiast
SCIENCE.summ: July 13, 2026 (Vilafranca del Penedès, Barcelona) , Bodegas Pinord has further strengthened the international reputation of organic and biodynamic viticulture in the Penedès after its Lluna Plena Biodynamic Chardonnay
takeaways: Bodegas Pinord's Lluna Plena Biodynamic Chardonnay received a high score from Wine Enthusiast.
The wine comes from Penedès, a region in Barcelona known for organic and biodynamic production.
The recognition reinforces Penedès' standing among world-class white wine regions.

SCIENCE.head: Grape Sunburn Spreads as Climate Change Reaches New Wine Regions
SCIENCE.summ: As climate change makes heat waves more prevalent, more producers have to combat sunburn in grapes, and the flabby, flat, rubbery notes they can bring to wine
takeaways: Direct sun exposure can push berry surface temperatures 22-27°F above ambient air, meaning a 95°F day creates surfaces exceeding 122°F on sun-facing sides.
Traditional mid-summer leaf removal now increases sunburn risk during heat waves; early-season defoliation allows berries to build photoprotective compounds, reducing damage.
Kaolin clay particle film reduces canopy temperatures by up to 9°C; some producers have eliminated summer leaf removal on white varieties entirely.
Sunburned grapes produce cooked and oxidized flavors that worsen with aging, and producers increasingly rely on harvest sorting as a final defense.

SCIENCE.head: French heatwave cuts Champagne yields 10%, triggers earliest harvest ever
SCIENCE.summ: France's prolonged summer heat and drought are shrinking grape development across major wine regions, with Champagne growers expecting yields to fall around 10% and harvests beginning weeks earlier than normal
takeaways: Champagne producers forecast yields roughly 10% below last year due to prolonged heat and drought.
Champagne harvest expected to begin around August 15, potentially the earliest on record.
Burgundy and Bordeaux also facing significant output declines; precise losses still uncertain.
Extended heat and water scarcity are increasing sugar concentration in grapes, potentially altering alcohol levels and flavor.
Champagne houses may offset lower yields using reserve wines held in stock.

SCIENCE.head: Heat-Tolerant Mourvèdre Struggles as Southern France Temperatures Rise
SCIENCE.summ: In a warming climate, even one of the world's most heat-tolerant grape varieties is reaching its limits
takeaways: Mourvèdre, traditionally grown in Bandol's coastal region, now faces challenges maintaining balance despite heat tolerance as temperatures and drought intensity increase.
Producers are adopting canopy management techniques—including higher gobelet training and three-dimensional shade systems—to protect grapes from sunburn.
Drought is forcing reconsideration of dry-farming practices; some Bandol growers now irrigate to prevent vine death, risking changes to regional terroir character.
Jumilla's vineyard surface area halved over 20 years as hotter southern sites were abandoned; remaining production concentrates on higher-altitude parcels with cooler conditions.
Climate shifts are reshaping wine styles and regional production: Bandol may reduce rosé output as cooler rosé parcels are converted to red wine production.

SCIENCE.head: Organic Sauvignon Blanc demands careful vineyard work to overcome disease sensitivity
SCIENCE.summ: From fruity and tropical to stony and herbal, there's a Sauvignon style for everyone, if you know where to look. These organic examples are perfect for summer
takeaways: Sauvignon Blanc is complex to grow organically because it's vulnerable to powdery and downy mildew, requiring frequent treatments.
Copper-based fungicides, the primary permitted organic option against mildew, can damage Sauvignon Blanc's sensitive flowers.
Biodynamic farming builds living soil that helps vines overcome disease and climate stress, according to practitioners in France and Austria.
Years of organic farming can increase Sauvignon Blanc's natural disease resilience, as demonstrated by growers in southern France.
Organic Sauvignon Blancs range from mineral and grassy styles to tropical fruit-forward expressions depending on terroir and winemaking methods.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Video: visiting an agroforestry coffee plantation in Brazil, the 15 hectare Sombra experiment that’s a collaboration between Fazenda Mió and Volcano Coffee Works in the UK
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Coffee is often grown as a monocrop. Here, at Fazenda Mió in Brazil I’m visiting a 15 hectare agroforestry experiment,
NEWSLETTERS.author: Jamie Goode
takeaways: Fazenda Mió in Brazil operates a 15-hectare agroforestry coffee experiment in collaboration with UK's Volcano Coffee Works.
Four coffee varieties are interplanted with three types of tree to provide shade and enhance soil life.
Cover crops between coffee rows boost soil microbiology and protect the soil.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Wine News: What I’m Reading the Week of 7/12/26
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Hello and welcome to my weekly roundup of the wine stories that I find of interest on the web. I
NEWSLETTERS.author: Alder Yarrow

NEWSLETTERS.head: Mountains and Magliocco
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Calabria might be known as the land of wine, but it depends where you go
NEWSLETTERS.author: Simon J. Woolf
takeaways: Calabria has nine scattered DOCs; only Cirò in the east has achieved fame, despite vines grown there since before the Romans.
Magliocco comes in two variants: Magliocco Dolce (more tannic, eastern Calabria) and Magliocco Canino (retains acidity, extremely late-ripening, possibly related to Sangiovese).
Casa Comerci, a certified organic estate near Nicotera, grows only Magliocco Canino and Greco Bianco, fermenting and aging exclusively in stainless steel, never barrels.
Magliocco Canino can ripen as late as early December; Casa Comerci's 2017 rosé was harvested in the first week of December.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Too Difficult?
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Yeats entitled one of his mid-life poems ‘The Fascination of What’s Difficult’. The poem is mainly a complaint, about the tangled business of running a theatre (the Abbey in Dublin) and the infuriating difficulty of “management of men”, as opposed to the pure delight of writing poetry. But the title points to something which has
NEWSLETTERS.author: Tim Atkin MW
takeaways: Assyrtiko, Greece's assertive grape variety, is high in acidity with texture and volume, dividing wine drinkers sharply in taste tests.
Orange wines by Joska Gravner of Friuli lack primary fruit notes and florality, requiring different appreciation than conventional white wines.
Nebbiolo-based Piedmont reds combine pale color with tannic structure and earthy, smoky notes rather than berry fruit flavors.
Sherry's deliberate oxidation and high acetaldehyde levels create a pungent character that initially deters many tasters but rewards persistence.

NEWSLETTERS.head: The Sysco-ification of Wine
NEWSLETTERS.summ: How wine culture's lazy "drink what you like" ethos has failed, and why it's time to break out the pitchforks
NEWSLETTERS.author: Jason Wilson
takeaways: 71 percent of U.S. wine is controlled by the top 11 suppliers, according to 2024 data from the American Association of Wine Economists.
Gallo controlled 33 percent of all wine in the U.S. market in 2023 and has since acquired Rombauer, Hahn Family Wines, Massican, Whiny Baby, and Bev canned wine.
Independent wine distributors fell from roughly 3,000 in the 1990s to just over 1,000 today.
Three dominant distributors—Breakthru, RNDC, and Southern Glazer's—controlled 53 percent of wine and spirits distribution in America as of 2024.
